content.problemStatement:
A number NNN is said to be XXX-valid if NNN is even, and all numbers from {1,2,...,N}\{1, 2, ..., N\}{1,2,...,N} can be split into N2\frac{N}{2}2Nâ€‹ mutually exclusive pairs of 222, where each pair has a sum which is a power of XXX.

Given NNN and XXX, find the number of integers iii satisfying 1â‰¤iâ‰¤N1 \le i \le N1â‰¤iâ‰¤N and iii is XXX-valid.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of a single line of input.

The first line of input contains NNN and XXX - the parameters defined in the statement.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the number of XXX-valid numbers iii such that 1â‰¤iâ‰¤N1 \le i \le N1â‰¤iâ‰¤N.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 1 : It can be proven that 222 and 666 are 333-valid numbers.


Construction for 222 : Divide {1,2}\{1, 2\}{1,2} into {(1,2)}\{(1, 2)\}{(1,2)}. Thus sum of the only pair in this list is 333, which is a trivial power of 333.


Construction for 666 : Divide {1,2,3,4,5,6}\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}{1,2,3,4,5,6} into {(1,2),(3,6),(4,5)}\{(1, 2), (3, 6), (4, 5)\}{(1,2),(3,6),(4,5)}. The sums of the pairs are {3,9,9}\{3, 9, 9\}{3,9,9} all of which are powers of 333.


It can be proven that there are exactly 777 numbers â‰¤27\le 27â‰¤27 which are 333-valid.
Test Case 3 : 44 4 is 555-valid while 111, 222 and 333 are not.
